coredata.dc:title: The use and detection of quantum dots as nanotracers in environmental fate studies of engineered nanoparticles

Part, Florian
coredata.dc:description: Investigations of the behavior and effects of engineered nanoparticles (ENPs) on human health and the environment need detailed knowledge of their fate and transport in environmental compartments. Such studies are highly challenging due to low environmental concentrations, varying size distribution of the particles and the interference with the natural background. A strategy to overcome these limits is to use mimics of ENPs with unique detectable properties that match the properties of the ENPs as nanotracers. A special class of ENPs that can be tracked are quantum dots (QDs). QDs are composed of different metals, metalloids, or more recently also carbon (e.g., graphene), that result in unique optical properties. This allows the tracking of such particles by fluorescence microscopic and photometric techniques. Many types of QDs consist of heavy elements, allowing to track and visualize these particles also by electron microscopy and to quantitate the particles indirectly based on these elements. QDs can also be surface modified in various ways which enable them to be used as a label or as traceable mimics for ENPs. This review reflects a broad range of methods to synthesize and modify QDs based on metals, metalloids, and graphene for studying the environmental fate of nanoparticles and discusses and compares analytical methods that can be used for tracking and quantifying QDs. In addition, we review applications of QDs as ENP mimics in environmental studies of surface waters, soils, microorganisms, and plants with respect to the applied analytical techniques.

head.ce:title: The use and detection of quantum dots as nanotracers in environmental fate studies of engineered nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Investigations of the behavior and effects of engineered nanoparticles (ENPs) on human health and the environment need detailed knowledge of their fate and transport in environmental compartments. Such studies are highly challenging due to low environmental concentrations, varying size distribution of the particles and the interference with the natural background. A strategy to overcome these limits is to use mimics of ENPs with unique detectable properties that match the properties of the ENPs as nanotracers. A special class of ENPs that can be tracked are quantum dots (QDs). QDs are composed of different metals, metalloids, or more recently also carbon (e.g., graphene), that result in unique optical properties. This allows the tracking of such particles by fluorescence microscopic and photometric techniques. Many types of QDs consist of heavy elements, allowing to track and visualize these particles also by electron microscopy and to quantitate the particles indirectly based on these elements. QDs can also be surface modified in various ways which enable them to be used as a label or as traceable mimics for ENPs. This review reflects a broad range of methods to synthesize and modify QDs based on metals, metalloids, and graphene for studying the environmental fate of nanoparticles and discusses and compares analytical methods that can be used for tracking and quantifying QDs. In addition, we review applications of QDs as ENP mimics in environmental studies of surface waters, soils, microorganisms, and plants with respect to the applied analytical techniques.

high resolution inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer
inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry and mass spectrometry

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles can have both natural and anthropogenic origin. Natural nanoparticles (NNPs) can be formed in all environmental compartments by chemical, photochemical, mechanical, thermal, and biological processes (Hochella et al., 2015; Hochella et al., 2019; Sharma et al., 2015). Nanoparticles of anthropogenic origin can be generated during processes that are not intended to produce nanoparticles (e.g. mining, combustion, etc.) (Miller et al., 2010; Sharma et al., 2015) and are called incidental nanoparticles (Westerhoff et al., 2018), or can intentionally be made and are called engineered nanoparticles (ENPs). The main difference between these classes of nanoparticles is the heterogeneity in composition and particle morphology, while ENPs are largely well defined and tailored in their properties. The demand for ENPs has increased in the past years, with the most produced ENP being made of SiO2 with up to 1.6 million tons per year and the lowest out of 24 different ENP classes being quantum dots (QDs) with 0.5–5 tons per year (Janković and Plata, 2019). The application areas of ENPs span today from cosmetics, such as nano-ZnO and -TiO2 for sun protection (Catalano et al., 2021), to rubber fillers (e.g., nano-SiO2) in tires (Sun et al., 2018), and semiconducting quantum dots (QDs) in solar cells or display technologies (Gressler et al., 2022; Markna and Rathod, 2022). ENPs can be released along the life cycle of nano-enabled products or during the production, use or end-of-life phase and thus may lead to environmental pollution (Koivisto et al., 2017; Bundschuh et al., 2018; Part et al., 2018). In addition, concerns were raised on the safety of such small particles in the size range of 1–100 nm, as ENPs can consist of toxic components (e.g. heavy metals), can exhibit photoreactive or catalytic properties, and can overcome biological barriers (Simkó and Mattsson, 2010; Miller et al., 2017). These particles possess different risk profiles and complex mechanisms respecting their toxicity compared to well-understood mechanisms of pure chemical elements and their soluble forms. Direct field measurements of ENPs are challenging due to their low environmental concentrations, the complex transformation processes, and the relative high occurrence of NNPs (e.g. SiO2 or TiO2), which makes modeling yet the best tool for evaluating the environmental fate and determining the predicted environmental concentration (PEC) of ENPs (Garner et al., 2017; Giese et al., 2018; Wagner et al., 2014). Therefore, laboratory studies on ENPs for investigating physical and chemical transformation processes under environmental conditions and obtaining toxicity data are necessary to provide information for improving environmental models (Wigger et al., 2020) and facilitating environmental risk assessment (Svendsen et al., 2020). However, even controllable laboratory settings can be challenging when the matrix is complex (e.g. waste water) and at the same time there is a lack of reference materials and standardized protocols (Johnston et al., 2020). A strategy to study ENPs in such complex matrices is to use tracers or mimics of these ENPs. These so-called nanotracers must exhibit unique properties and fingerprints that aid their quantification and identification. An important class of such nanoparticles that can be used as nanotracers are QDs, which can be synthesized in diameters between 1 and 6 nm and have unique size-dependent and tunable optical properties (Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Part et al., 2016; Murray et al., 2018). QDs were intensively studied in the past years, and therefore many methods and protocols have become available that allow to modify their surface properties (e.g., Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Sperling and Parak, 2010; Sapsford et al., 2013; Bilan et al., 2015). This variety of protocols for a broad range of ENPs allow to mimic the surface properties that has a strong impact on the mobility, transport, and fate of ENPs with similar physical-chemical properties.

Many articles were published in recent years that report the synthesis, modification, analysis, application, safety, and environmental behavior of QDs (Fig. 1 A). However, in the early 2010 new classes of QDs were discovered and therefore the first discovered class of QDs was referred to semiconductor quantum dots (SQD), while the newly found classes were perovskite QDs, carbon QDs (CQDs), and graphene QDs (GQDs). The numbers of publications of SQDs are stagnating while perovskite QDs, CQDs, and GQDs are intensively studied, which could indicate that the material will be introduced into commercial products in the next years (Fig. 1 B). SQDs typically consist of metal chalcogenide, while perovskite QDs are made from halide perovskite materials (Chen and Chen, 2019), which can be composed of cesium (Cs), lead (Pb) cadmium (Cd), selenium (Se), tellurium (Te), zinc (Zn), sulfur (S), indium (In), gallium (Ga), or phosphorus (P) – and are generally classified into group II-VI, IV-VI, and III-V elements, with Cd being the dominant element used in SQDs (Fig. 1 C) (Tarantini et al., 2019). Both perovskite and chalcogenide QDs are used for biomedical research (Ballou et al., 2004; Lian et al., 2022), but only the chalcogenide QDs were used so far as nanotracers for environmental fate studies. In contrast to SQDs, CQDs and GQDs have recently been discovered and synthesized. CQDs provide outstanding fluorescent properties with sizes below 10 nm (Wang and Hu, 2014). GQDs are also fluorescent and consist of a single to few graphene layers with dimensions of less than 20 nm (Chung et al., 2021). On the application side, SQDs are still intensively studied as material for LEDs, perovskite QDs for LEDs and solar power applications, CQDs primarily for solar power applications, and GQDs in all fields including biomedical applications (Fig. 1 D). The most advantageous property of GQDs is that they consist mostly of carbon, rather than heavy metals as in SQDs (Meierhofer et al., 2020). Therefore, GQDs are more environmentally friendly and more sustainable than SQDs. However, health and environmental risks of carbon based QDs are not well understood so far.

However, for a successful and safe application of any ENP type, it is important to study their behavior, transport, and fate in the environment to understand the physical-chemical properties of the particles and the limitations of such studies regarding transformation processes and detectability. In addition, this information can also be derived to understand the processes involving NNPs or anthropogenic incidental nanoparticles. This review provides a portfolio of appropriate QDs, surface modifications and detection methods that were used to study the environmental behavior of ENPs in surface waters, soils, microorganisms, and plants.

The literature search (27.09.2022) for publications on QD synthesis, surface modifications, and applications was conducted with SciFindern by applying searches on abstracts of articles using the keyword “quantum dot” for the sum of QD articles (Fig. 1 A). For the discrimination between CQDs, GQDs, SQDs, and perovskite QDs the terms “carbon quantum dot”, “graphene quantum dot”, “semiconductor quantum dot”, and “perovskite quantum dot” were used (Fig. 1 B) and filtered for the range of years 2001–2021. Using the abbreviations returned less results than the full term with 33% for QD, 1% for CQD, 4% GQD, 7% for SQD, and 21% for perovskite QD. However, using the plural form of any of the terms returned the same number of articles as the singular form. The main elements of SQDs and perovskite QDs were obtained from the publications that were assessed in Section 3 and were used by combining the terms “quantum dot” and the abbreviation of the element and cumulated for the years 2001–2021 (Fig. 1C). The search terms biomedical, solar, and LED were combined with the single quantum dot terms using “AND” and cumulated for the years 2001–2022 (Fig. 1 D).

The literature searches on analytical methods that are used to identify, quantify, and characterize QDs was made in Google Scholar (search date 31.01.2022) based on analytical techniques that were identified in publications that were reviewed for QD properties and case studies. Google Scholar was chosen for its ability of full text search, which is necessary, as analytical techniques are mostly mentioned in the material and methods section of publications, rather than in the abstract, title, or keywords, and is not covered by the search parameters of other scientific search engines (such as Web of Knowledge, Web of Science, or Scopus). Google Scholar has the limitation that the search results are not absolute but depend on the search time (that cannot be selected by the user), which results in different hit numbers when the same keywords are used again. However, the magnitude of the results remains the same. Moreover, hits in Google Scholar appear to be estimations, as only the first 3 digits of the hit number are exactly given, and the rest of the digits are displayed as zeros. Nevertheless, the method is still useful as the magnitudes are maintained over multiple searches and allow an estimation on the frequency of the analytical methods that were used. The precise term ‘quantum dot’ or alternatively ‘quantum dots’ served as search parameter, as the abbreviation ‘QD’ was often misinterpreted with the abbreviation of first and middle name of authors. We found more hits for the plural term ‘quantum dots’ (QDs; 1.6 × 106 hits, 100%) than for singular term ‘quantum dot’ (QD; 0.95 × 106 hits, 59%), which is shown in Fig. 5 as ‘sum’. For assessing the number of publications that also mention a method, the term ‘quantum dot’ or ‘quantum dots’ was separately combined with the method acronym via an AND condition, which was abbreviated as “QD” and “QDs” in Fig. 5, respectively.

For the search of case studies on QD-based nanotracers in the environment, search engines and databases for scientific literature, such as PubMed, Science Direct, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were used to find peer-reviewed literature. For the literature search, the keywords “quantum dot(s)”, “plant(s)”, “tracking” and “fate” were mainly used. The case studies do not claim to be complete but are intended to show representative examples of such environmental relevant studies.

Photoluminescence (PL) is the property of any substance to emit light upon absorption of photons. Both SQDs and GQDs exhibit PL properties, but the origin of PL in both materials is different and still subject to research in the case of GQDs (Cayuela et al., 2016; Rakovich and Rakovich, 2018). A comparison of the properties between SQDs and GQDs is given in Table 1 . Fig. 2 A, which displays the principle of PL in bulk semiconductors, and SQDs. Photons with energies that are higher than the bandgap of the material elevate an excited electron from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) leaving an electron hole behind in the VB, which is called the exciton (Quintanilla et al., 2018). When the electron and hole recombine a photon with the bandgap energy (Ebg) is emitted as fluorescence radiation. With decreasing size of the material, the continuum becomes more discrete and the bandgap larger (Quintanilla et al., 2018). The number and energy of these discrete levels depend on the particle size which means that the absorption and emission behavior of such ENPs can be tuned by varying their sizes (Himmelstoβ and Hirsch, 2019). Smaller particle sizes result in a wider bandgap and a stronger blue shift of the emitted light (Fig. 2 A) (see Fig. 3 ).

In the case of GQDs, the origin of their luminescent properties is more elaborate. A perfect sheet of pristine graphene represents a zero bandgap semiconductor material that does not exhibit PL properties (Cao et al., 2013). Therefore, graphene must be modified to introduce a bandgap and induce fluorescence in graphene-based materials. To tune the band structure of graphene, two approaches have been pursued. One approach is to cut graphene into nano-sized sheets or to add functional groups to create isolated areas (Galande et al., 2011). However, these modifications give rise to different PL origins which can be tuned differently. Current research suggests that two distinct origins of PL in GQDs. The first type of PL originates from sp2-domains (conjugated π-domains) (Cao et al., 2013) that are isolated by defects in the graphene sheet or on the edges, or by oxygen-bearing functional groups (Jang et al., 2017). Inducing PL by creating isolated sp2-domains is conceptually like PL observed in SQDs since the GQDs’ PL can be tuned by controlling the size, shape, and fraction of the sp2-domains. With respect to this type of PL, it is appropriate to categorize this material as QD since the quantum confinement effect leads to discrete energy levels, while larger bandgaps are observed for smaller-sized sp2-domains (Fig. 2 B) (Eda et al., 2010). The second type of PL in GQDs originates from functionalized surface defect sites which result from the oxidation of graphene sheets and thereby adding oxygen-bearing, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, or hydroxyl groups (Galande et al., 2011). Therefore, depending on the fraction of sp2-domains and the degree of oxidation, the PL of GQDs is composed of both bandgap (sp2-domains) and non-bandgap (defects) emissions.

The photoluminescence quantum yield (ΦPL, or PLQY), together with the molar extinction coefficient, are key performance indicators to evaluate the fluorescence performance of ENPs for tracer studies. The ΦPL is defined as the ratio of the number of photons emitted and the number of photons absorbed by the probe which is a measure for the conversion efficiency of absorbed photons into emitted light (Würth et al., 2015). Determining ΦPL provides information about nanocrystal quality, and stability, as well as their performance in analytical methods detecting fluorescence properties (detection limit, dynamic range, etc.) (Abbandonato et al., 2018). The ΦPL of QDs is typically determined optically, relative to the ΦPL of a dye (e.g., Rhodamine 6G), whose absorption and emission behavior is closely related to that of the studied PL emitter, which is less elaborate than its absolute determination by photothermal methods (Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Abbandonato et al., 2018; Chung et al., 2021). The ΦPL of SQDs typically are 40–70% (VIS) and 20–40% (NIR), whereas ΦPL of GQDs are significantly lower at 5–25% (VIS).

SQDs and GQDs can absorb UV and visible light while the absorbance gradually increases with decreasing wavelengths (Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Zheng and Wu, 2017). The absorption and emission behavior of both QD types is modifiable and depends on different parameters: The photonic properties of SQDs depend on core, shell, capping, and alloying/doping materials (Darbandi et al., 2010; Algar et al., 2011; Tarantini et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2021), particle size and size distribution (Rogach et al., 2000; Resch-Genger et al., 2008; Heyne et al., 2021), and shell thickness (Darbandi et al., 2010; Heyne et al., 2020). In contrast to SQDs, tuning of GQDs’ photonic properties is mainly controlled by the size of the sp2-domains which is controlled by chemical modification or simple fragmentation to small platelets.

Symmetric and narrow emission bands (small full width half maximum, FWHM) are characteristic for most SQDs while the width of the emission bands is a function of the particle size distribution (Resch-Genger et al., 2008). In contrast, GQDs exhibit typically asymmetric broad emission peaks combined with excitation-dependent behavior originating from their “quasi-molecular” electronic structure (Zhu et al., 2011; Rakovich and Rakovich, 2018). Jang et al. (2017) attributed the shape of GQDs emission peaks to the different PL origins. Emission peaks originating from sp2-domains (intrinsic states) show narrow linewidths with higher energy, while emission peaks originating from oxygen-functional groups (extrinsic states) are generally broader. Furthermore, GQDs that were investigated under acidic conditions (suppressing extrinsic states) and those GQDs, which lacked sp2-domains, did not show excitation-dependent PL behavior (Jang et al., 2017). Therefore, functional groups on the edges or doping of GQDs (e.g., to enhance their ΦPL) can change the PL shape (Rakovich and Rakovich, 2018).

An important property of fluorescent materials is the so-called Stokes shift, which is defined as the difference between excitation- and emission wavelength, whereas the characteristic excitation wavelength of QDs is defined as the maximum of the first excitonic peak. Large Stokes shifts are favorable since small shifts can lead to self-quenching and errors in measurements with poor signal-to-noise ratios, poor detection sensitivity, and low ΦPL (Peng et al., 2005; Ren et al., 2018; Bae et al., 2021). Tunable Stokes shifts together with narrow and ideally well-separated emission bands are desirable characteristics for QDs in multiplexing detection at single excitation wavelengths to avoid crosstalk, as well as to enable a simple color discrimination (Resch-Genger et al., 2008).

Both SQDs and GQDs were shown to be highly photostable materials that withstand loss of photoluminescence upon long-lasting excitation which is a great advantage over most organic dyes. With respect to SQDs, photooxidation leads to a shrinkage of the optically active core, which in turn results in blue-shifted emissions and eventually in the loss of PL caused by photobleaching (van Sark et al., 2001). Shells can shield the core against oxygen and leaching of the metallic core materials to a certain degree, depending on the compounds used for surface passivation, with silica being reported as being a stronger oxygen barrier than, for example, ZnS or polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) (van Sark et al., 2001; Iso and Isobe, 2020). However, too thin shells result in a reduced photostability due to the inefficient passivation of the fluorescent core (Reiss et al., 2009).

Nanomaterials exhibit large surface-to-volume ratios and consequently controlling the surface chemistry plays an important role in their environmental behavior. An overview on the extensive possible compositions and surface modifications of SQDs is sketched in Fig. 4 . Surface modifications can be used to change physical properties of SQDs like ΦPL, bandgap, electron affinity, ionization potential etc. by applying different surface ligands (Boles et al., 2016). The broad variety of surface modifications is given in Table 2 . Surface ligands – also termed organic coatings – attach to the SQDs’ surface and build a capping layer around the QD, which shields the nanocrystal from its environment, saturates dangling bonds (unoccupied electron orbitals), and stabilizes the particles against aggregation and dissolution (Sperling and Parak, 2010; Zrazhevskiy et al., 2010). Therefore, the coating is critical for maintaining SQDs dispersible in aqueous environments and allows to undertake tracing studies (Resch-Genger et al., 2008). SQDs are often synthesized by high temperature methods using tri-n-octyl phosphine (TOP) or its oxide, tri-n-octyl phosphine oxide (TOPO), as stabilizers and result in high quality SQDs which are, however, hydrophobic and only dispersible in nonpolar organic solvents (Zrazhevskiy et al., 2010; Bilan et al., 2015). Aqueous synthesis routes also have been explored but typically SQDs exhibit relatively low ΦPL after synthesis (Liu et al., 2018). Therefore, exchanging the surface ligands is a way to make high quality SQDs dispersible in polar solvents, which is necessary if SQDs are applied as nanotracers in aqueous environments, such as groundwater bodies, wastewater or landfill leachates (Part et al., 2016; Part et al., 2020). The basic strategies to turn hydrophobic SQDs hydrophilic are ligand exchange, or encapsulation in a heterofunctional organic coating (Medintz et al., 2005). A widely used substance class for ligand exchange reactions are mercaptocarboxylic acids with varying alkane or polyethylenglycol (PEG) spacers, such as mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), which consist of hydrophilic carboxylate groups at one tail and a thiol group that binds to the QD surface on the other. Using thiol groups is the most prominent strategy to make SQDs water-dispersible owing to its speed and simplicity. However, the QD's thiol bonds are not stable in the long term and can detach over time, makeing SQDs susceptible to aggregation (Smith et al., 2006). Other thiol-based ligands are, for example, dihydrolipoic acid (has two thiol groups) (Liu et al., 2008), (±)-2-amino-3-mercaptopropionic acid (DL-cysteine) (Sukhanova et al., 2004), or mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane (Gerion et al., 2001). In case of encapsulation, the addition of amphiphilic molecules to the SQD dispersion causes an intercalation of the hydrophobic chains in the QD surface layers while the hydrophilic groups are directed outwards, thereby stabilizing the modified QDs in polar solvents (Bilan et al., 2015). While encapsulation produces highly stable QDs and preserves their optical properties, a shortcoming of this method is the considerable increase of the hydrodynamic diameter of the particle (Zrazhevskiy et al., 2010). A method of encapsulation is to grow a silica shell around an SQDs core. The silica shell is chemically inert, optically transparent, water-dispersible, and can be synthesized with a controlled porosity, which significantly reduces leakage of toxic elements (Darbandi et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013; Goftman et al., 2016; Murase et al., 2019). This approach would also allow to create traceable mimics of industrially produced nano-SiO2.

For GQDs, graphene oxide is used as the preferred precursor material due to its oxygen-bearing groups which allow facile covalent and non-covalent functionalizations (Xu et al., 2017). At the same time, a shortcoming that has to be considered and overcome in view of synthesizing GQDs is their compositional heterogeneity, which is considered as an obstacle for using GQDs for practical applications (Bradley et al., 2017). Regarding water-dispersibility, post-synthesis modifications of GQDs are generally not required, since – depending on the synthesis route – GQDs intrinsically feature various degrees of hydrophilic oxygen-bearing functional groups on the basal plane or edges (Chung et al., 2021). Coating with silica shells is also possible for GQDs and adds similar benefits as for SQDs, such as chemical stability, stability towards leakage and prevention of aggregation, as well as improved photostability (Wang et al., 2015). Incorporating other elements like nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), or boron (B) in the carbon lattice of GQDs is another strategy to modify optical properties, photostability, and fluorescence intermittency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots as mimics for ENPs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, mimics are applied in cases when the original material cannot be used, but certain properties need to be investigated. Therefore, the mimic will always exhibit limitations towards the original material. Reasons for using ENP mimics are limitations by lack of detectability, stability, or toxicity of the original ENP. QD-based mimics share some general properties with ENPs due to their small size and add unique features of detectability due to their fluorescence properties. Limitations arise from their limited size of 1–10 nm, by which larger particles cannot be mimicked, and their composition, which is limited to a few elements and determines the surface properties of pristine QDs. However, the size of QDs can be enlarged by growing silica shells around the particles’ core which is possible to a total diameter of more than 100 nm, and thus covering the whole defined size range of nanoparticles (Yang et al., 2012; Elzorkany et al., 2019; Jo et al., 2020). These QDs can readily mimic SiO2 ENPs, which are hardly detectable as Si is ubiquitous in environmental samples. Their detectability can be enhanced through the doping of less abundant elements (such as Cd, Se, etc.), or even rare stable isotopes (e.g., 111Cd). However, toxicity assessment is limited as in case of dissolution heavy metals can be leached from QDs. Additionally, methods for preparing silica coated QDs have to be carefully selected, as some methods lead not to entirely coated QDs, such as raise-bun particles (Rogach et al., 2000), and thus exhibit non-uniform silicate surfaces.

The colloidal stability of ENPs is quantitatively described by the DLVO theory (Derjaguin, Landau, Verwey, and Overbeek) in aqueous media for charged particles (Da Cruz Schneid et al., 2022; Tadros, 2007). According to the theory, the stability of nanoparticles is determined by attractive van der Waals and repulsive electrostatic interactions, which depend on surface properties and conditions of the environment (pH, ionic strength, electrolyte composition, temperature, etc.). These properties can be adapted to mimic the surface of the many ENPs by introducing organic ligands with appropriate modifications to QDs. Mimics of ENPs >10 nm, made from silica QD composites, can also be further modified by organic ligands using silane chemistry.

Despite the manifold possibilities of designing detectable QD-based ENP mimics, certain materials cannot be imitated today. The surface of many pristine ENPs, including some with the largest production volumes (TiO2, ZnO, Al2O3, CNT and others), cannot be constructed around QDs until synthesis strategies are found that allow the creation of composite materials similar to silica QD composites. For such materials, other labeling techniques could be used such as stable isotope labelling. A second limitation originates from the density of the QD materials when aggregation mechanisms are studied, as in such cases the colloidal stability is lifted and the particles sediment according to Stoke's law (Markus et al., 2015). Therefore, the challenge in such studies lies in tuning the density of the QD-based mimic to match the original material.

Characterization of pristine SQDs and GQDs is an important prerequisite for their use as nanotracers. QD characterizing properties are geometric diameter, size distribution in dispersion, excitation and emission wavelength, and the photoluminescence quantum yield (ΦPL or PLQY). In some cases, the elemental composition and the degree of crystallinity are also determined. Surface properties, such as the surface charge state or the presence and extent of ligands are also often required. The characterization of pristine and transformed QDs is part of the field of nanoscience and often involves expensive and not readily useable instrumentation that cannot be readily applied for complex environmental samples. Due to their strengths and weaknesses, each technique alone is usually not sufficient for identifying and quantifying nanoparticles and therefore a combination of these techniques is necessary to obtain sufficient information (Part et al., 2015). A summary of analytical techniques that were used to characterize or to track SQDs and GQDs is given in Table 3 , with references to the field studies, where these techniques were used and described in more detail. A general review on analyses of ENPs in complex samples is given by, for example, Laborda et al. (2016) and for non-microscopic techniques by Xie et al. (2022).

The most commonly used method to determine the geometric size, which represents the size of the core and shell materials without the ligands, of QDs is transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM can be used to determine size and size distribution, as well as crystallinity, or also chemical composition when it is coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Walther, 2015). The resolution of TEM can be expanded to atomic resolution, when high resolution TEM is applied (HR-TEM), which is usually operated at higher voltages and useful for visualizing GQDs (Wu et al., 2013; Ge et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2016; Bradley et al., 2017; Tachi et al., 2019). Scanning TEM (STEM) is mainly offering advantages for imaging large areas (Peckys et al., 2014). TEM can return detailed information on individual particles, but only small fractions can be measured. All TEM techniques require clean and homogeneous samples, which are typically transferred as a dispersion to TEM grids. The usual volumes that can be transferred are limited to 1–10 μl and only a small fraction of the particles will bind to the foil on the grid. It is important to find an optimal method for preparing the TEM grid, as in too high dilution only a few particles will be found per image, while highly concentrated dispersions can cause multi-layered depositions (Boiko et al., 2020). In cases of low concentrations, multiple blotting can also be an option. During the drying process, aggregation of the particles can happen, which is caused by the formation of microdroplets that become smaller during the evaporation of the dispersion medium. This effect is observed when the dispersion medium is polar with a poor wettability on the hydrophobic film on the grid. The interaction can be reduced by using polar films or oxidizing the film in an ozonizer. For TEM, particles must be separated from environmental matrices as TEM will give good contrast also for inorganic matrix materials.

The second technique that is used to determine the geometric diameter of QDs is atomic force microscopy (AFM), which was mentioned in 7% and 5% of all literature hits (108 × 103 and 49.9 × 103 QDs and QD, respectively). AFM is a mechanical microscope that uses a micrometric cantilever with a nanometric tip that scratches or vibrates over a flat surface that is moved by a piezo stage with nanometric precision. During the scanning process, the bending of the tip is used to determine the surface height at subatomic resolution. Therefore, atomic force micrographs also return the height of each particle, which is not possible by TEM. However, AFM is more complicated to use than standard TEM, as a nanoscopic, atomically flat and rigid substrate, such as mica, single crystals, or silicon wafers, is required. In addition, the particles should also interact strongly with the surface as they can otherwise be moved mechanically by the tip and would not be imaged. The substrate is often modified to increase the interaction by e.g. silanes (Rao et al., 2007). In addition, AFM needs far longer for each measurement and must be manually readjusted often. Another drawback is that the tips of the cantilever can wear rapidly or become contaminated, which can cause a loss of image quality and consequently also wrong size measurements. Therefore, AFM is used only in a few cases with pristine QDs and is generally not applicable in heterogenous environmental matrices.

All quantum dots exhibit spectroscopic properties in the UV, VIS, or NIR region. The absorption of photons is determined by UV/VIS or UV/VIS/NIR spectrometers, which also serves to determine the excitons of QDs. These types of measurements are usually made in dispersions and necessary to determine the spectroscopic properties, such as the absorbance wavelengths of QDs. The terms UV, VIS, and NIR were found in 19%, 10%, and 8% of all search hits for QDs (13%, 8%, and 6% for QD). The absorbance of QDs can be tracked in environmental samples by detecting the specific wavelength of the excitonic peak when the matrix does not absorb (Part et al., 2016; Part et al., 2020).

Fluorescence spectroscopy is also used (2% for QDs and 1% for QD) to characterize the ideal excitation wavelength and is based on the emission. This technique can also be used to quantify QDs in an environmental sample, when the sample does not exhibit a background fluorescence (e.g. by proteins or humic acids). The PLQY is also determined by fluorescence spectroscopy, usually by using a fluorescence dye standard with a known quantum yield for correcting the instrument parameters (Würth et al., 2012; Zaba et al., 2016). A direct determination can be made by fluorescence lifetime measurements with a pulsed laser source, which are not standard instruments and are only useful for the characterization of the pristine material (Miao et al., 2018). More advanced fluorescence microscopy techniques, which reach higher spatial resolution and sensitivity, are confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy (TIRF). These microscopic techniques are not used for determining size, since the particles are far smaller than the resolution of these microscopes, but they are very useful for their localization, as it can be performed also in liquids and under atmospheric conditions. Fluorescence microscopes operate with laser or powerful UV lamps and LEDs as excitation source in combination with filters and CCD cameras or photomultiplier tubes. Using different lasers and filters also allow to localize different particles with different emission properties. However, the techniques can differ strongly, as e.g. TIRF is a very rapid technique but can be used only for fluorescent samples that are close to the surface (<1 μm) (Fish, 2009). In CLSM, on the other hand, each point of the sample can be analyzed, as the focal point can be moved in any direction, but each of these points is measured independently and therefore imaging is very time-consuming. Both techniques need glass supports and cannot be used when samples are moving, which makes them a good choice for immobilized samples. For environmental samples, these techniques can be useful to determine accumulations of particles in certain sub compartments of an environmental sample (e.g. in plants or microorganisms). Nevertheless, for such analyses, all compartments must be prepared independently and usually the accumulations are identified beforehand by elemental analysis, such as with ICP-based methods.

Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) mass spectrometry (MS) or optical emission spectroscopy (OES) are powerful analytical techniques to quantify chemical elements. While OES is less sensitive but cheaper in its maintenance, MS detectors can also distinguish isotopes, when used in HR-ICP-MS setups. These techniques can determine the quantities of many elements in one sample. However, the method was applied so far only on SQDs, but not on GQDs or CQDs, since carbon is ubiquitous in environmental samples. For using ICP-MS or ICP-OES, it must be considered that the sample must be digested in concentrated acids to solubilize all solid matter and destroy the material of interest and the matrix. Also, naturally occurring elements are problematic as they add a background signal. This means that the detected elements cannot easily be attributed to SQDs only. Problems with interfering background elements can be circumvented by using isotopically labelled SQDs, which are synthesized SQDs from educts that contain a high proportion of a rarely naturally occurring and stable isotope of the same element. Since stable isotopes exhibit the same chemical properties, the same synthesis methods as for non-isotopically labelled SQDs can be used (Supiandi et al., 2020a; Supiandi et al., 2020b). Such labelled QDs can be detected even at ppt levels in environmental samples by HR-ICP-MS. A disadvantage of the method is that it does not provide information on size distribution or surface coating. A special setup variant of this technique is single particle ICP-MS (spICP-MS) that also allows to determine the quantity and distribution of nanoparticles (Montaño et al., 2016). However, for such a measurement, the instrument must be calibrated with particle size standards and is limited to certain sizes of the particles (e.g. for Pb and Cd in the range of 11–20 nm) (Lee et al., 2014). Therefore sICP-MS is not currently applicable to smaller QDs.

Another technique that can be used to determine QDs in environmental samples is laser induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS). For this technique, a highly energetic laser source is used to destroy solid material and form a plasma (Jolivet et al., 2019). The plasma emits photons that are characteristic for the chemical elements that were ionized and can be used for quantification. Therefore, QDs are destroyed together with the matrix and can only be identified and quantified by their elements but does not provide information on the size of the QDs, their ligands, or transformation processes. In addition to ICP-based techniques, the laser can be focused to a few micrometers and can be also used to localize accumulations of QDs. However, LIBS is also limited to determine QDs that consist of non-ubiquitous chemical elements.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) is a technique that is frequently used for determining the hydrodynamic diameter (HDD), which is larger than the geometric diameter of ENPs as the measurements includes also the size of ligands as well as the hydration shell (Brar and Verma, 2011). In DLS, the autocorrelation of changing scattering patterns of a laser is determined and is used to calculate the sizes and size distribution of particles. In addition, DLS can be operated in some setups under electrochemical potential and can be used to measure the zeta potential, which is used to determine the charge of the particles. The stability of particles can also be investigated using DLS through changing prevailing conditions in the solution (e.g., pH, electrolyte composition) and measuring the change in size distribution. The measurements are performed in dispersion but cannot distinguish between ENPs and NNPs, and are prone to matrix effects.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) are used for analyzing ligands of QDs, and are primarily performed on QDs before they are subjected to environmental matrices (Bradley et al., 2017; Greaney and Brutchey, 2015; Wang et al., 2015). In TGA, a mass measurement is performed during heating of the sample. The mass losses can be interpreted as loss of ligands and as degree of grafting of those ligands on QDs. FTIR determines the presence of organic bonds, which originate usually from the ligands of QDs. However, TGA can only be used for solid samples, while some setups in FTIR allow measurements in liquids at the cost of sensitivity, as the solvents also exhibit strong absorptions in this spectral region. For such FTIR measurements in liquids, augmented total reflection (ATR) setups are mostly used, as those techniques only measure samples in close vicinity (few μm) to the ATR crystal surface and reduce background absorption of the solvents. In some cases, FTIR spectroscopy can be applied to obtain additional information on the presence, or absence of organic ligands to FTIR (Part et al., 2020).

Extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) is a powerful technique to analyze elements in a solid or liquid sample, by measuring the absorption of X-rays. The technique allows to analyze not only chemical elements but also the chemical environment of these elements and the atomic distances, which results in deeper information on attached ligands and the transformation of the QDs. However, for EXAFS, monochromatic X-rays are needed as excitation source that are only available at large facilities, such as synchrotrons. This makes EXAFS the most sophisticated technique that was used for the analysis of QDs in environmental samples so far (Frenkel, 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principals of environmental behavior of ENPs
ce:section.ce:para: Investigating the environmental behavior of ENPs is an analytical challenge due to low concentrations, the size of the particles and possible transformation processes. Such transformation processes of ENPs are summarized for the aquatic environment in Fig. 5. The strongest impact on the behavior is determined by the specific surface properties of the particles which mostly depend on their ligands. In nano-enabled products or components, ENPs are either immobilized in or on solid materials, or need to be colloidally stabilized in a dispersion media for avoiding aggregation (Reimhult, 2017). Colloidally stable particles are separated from each other, by charge or steric repulsion of the ligands on the particles. This repulsion can be lifted when the environmental conditions change, such as electrolyte composition, pH, and the presence of organic compounds (e.g., dissolved organic matter such as humic or fulvic acids). Homo-aggregation can take place when the dispersibility of the particles changes due to dynamic environmental conditions, while in case of hetero-aggregation, ENPs bind to components in the matrix, such as dissolved organic matter (DOM) or natural micro- and nanoparticles. ENPs can also react and form hetero-aggregates with natural abundant organic molecules from biological origin (such as polysaccharides, proteins, nucleic acids) and natural polymers resulting from decomposition (lignin and cellulose, and humic and fulvic acids) (Quigg et al., 2013; Markiewicz et al., 2018). Bioaccumulation is also possible by different pathways of transformation, such as enzymatic degradation or corona formation (Markiewicz et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020; Svendsen et al., 2020). In addition, the dissolution of particles and the subsequent release of ions is also possible by redox reactions with dissolved components or by photochemical processes (Rocha et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selected case studies to track QDs in the environment
ce:section.ce:para: Case studies were selected to highlight the application of QDs as nanotracers in most relevant terrestrial environmental compartments. The case studies cover selected parts of the environment that have not yet been examined in detail and do not cover objects of research already comprehensively reviewed, such as earthworms (Adeel et al., 2021) or aquatic organisms (Rocha et al., 2017). A summary of these case studies and the analytical techniques that were applied is given in Table 4 .

ENPs enter surface water bodies and are dissipated there by wastewater or after leaching from soil into groundwater. Due to their dilution, it is difficult to prepare experiments that investigate the fate of such particles at realistic environmental concentrations. Recently, Supiandi et al. (2020b) studied the behavior of CdSe/ZnS SQDs capped with thioglycolic acid in synthetic rivers at environmentally relevant concentrations at the ppt (parts per trillion; 10−12) level. For achieving this analytical sensitivity, the authors used multi-isotopically labelled 111Cd77Se/68ZnS SQDs, which can also be distinguished from high natural background concentrations of Cd and Zn, and separated the particles by centrifugal ultrafiltration from the matrix before detecting the particles by HR-ICP-MS (Supiandi et al., 2019; Supiandi et al., 2020a). The authors emphasized that the ENP concentration and the aggregation state can have a major impact on dissolution kinetics and thus on ENP behavior in aquatic environments (Supiandi et al., 2019). Synthetic river water was prepared using different concentrations of Ca(NO3)2, Na2SO4, NaHEPES salts, goethite (α-Fe3+O(OH)) and Suwannee River fulvic acid (5 ppm) to mimic different water conditions of the river Seine in Paris. Scanned stripping chronopotentiometry was used to quantify the overall Zn and Cd concentration in solution. Combining these techniques allowed the determination of the SQD fate at ppt concentrations (LOD ca. 5 ppt). The laboratory scale experiments showed that almost no Zn or Cd was liberated from the QDs, as the thioglycolic acid ligands sufficiently passivated the metallic SQD cores, even when Zn, fulvic acid, and goethite were present in the matrix. When DOM and natural minerals were absent, the SQDs’ ligands detached with time and thus particle dissolution began. The authors concluded that the mechanism of dissolution could not have been discovered without the high sensitivity of the method.

An example for wastewater that can also enter water bodies uncontrolled are landfill leachates (Part et al., 2020). Part et al. investigated the behavior of hydrophilic NAC-capped CdTe and surfactant-stabilized (Brij-58) CdSe/ZnS in leachates from landfill sites for municipal solid waste, construction and bulky wastes. The SQDs were traced over a period of 180 days by UV/VIS and fluorescence spectroscopy. FTIR was used to investigate the degradation of organic coatings in the leachate samples. The leachates exhibited complex composition of several ions (e.g., 71–209 ppm Na, 3–8 ppm Ca, 19–34 ppm Mg, 4–7 ppt Zn, 0.4 ppt Cd), humic, and fulvic acids (7–109 ppm DOC). NAC-QDs showed a loss of their short and covalently bound ligands with time, followed by the liberation of dissolved Zn and Cd ions. On the contrary, Brij-QDs underwent sedimentation, lost the surfactants and the ligands at later points but maintained their persistence in the sediments. In the case of NAC-QDs, ligand detachment and particle dissolution were decelerated at low temperatures, while water-dispersibility remained highest in the leachates with lowest electrolyte and DOC contents. The behavior in the different leachates, showed that the NAC-QDs are more subject to the stabilizing mechanisms according to the DLVO theory and the Brij-QDs are more subject to steric repulsion. The authors used the absorbance and fluorescence signals to determine the mobile fraction of the SQDs and the detachment of ligands, which was only possible by using high concentrations of SQDs. The study confirmed that the used capping agents and temperature as well as the environmental conditions are the key factors in environmental behavior of ENPs. It is stressed that landfill leachates are generally a challeging matrix because they have complex and very different electrolyte and DOC composition, depending primarily on the type and age of the landfill.
ENPs can enter soil when they are used as nano-agrochemicals, when ENP-containing products are damaged, or when ENP-containing sludges from wastewater treatment are used as fertilizer.

Torkzaban et al. (2013) investigated the transport and behavior of poly acrylic acid (PAA)-capped CdTe SQDs in saturated soil columns that contained sand with an average grain size of 270 μm. The columns were flushed at a constant average pore-water velocity of 7 m per day using deionized water which was spiked with 2 mM Ca, 1–100 mM Na salts, and was buffered at pH 8. The results from UV/VIS and ICP-MS revealed that the carboxyl groups of the SQDs’ ligands and divalent ions played the key role in ENP mobility. In the absence of Ca, the mobility was less inhibited, which was attributed to the lack of bridging of the carboxylic groups.

Navarro et al. (2011) studied the mobility of MPA-capped CdSe and CdSe/ZnS SQDs in saturated small-scaled soil columns. Both SQD types and dissolved components (i.e., Zn, Cd and Se) were retained by the topsoil after leaching the column. The ICP-MS results of the leachates indicated that SQDs had limited mobility in the topsoil even under conditions that simulated heavy rain. However, when EDTA was added as chelating agent both intact SQDs and liberated dissolved Cd and Zn species could be mobilized. The authors observed that the SQDs' organic ligands were vulnerable to degradation (including the dissolution of the QD's metallic core) unless NOM or chelating agents were present that re-mobilize SQDs.

Microorganisms are an integral component of all ecosystems and play key roles in primary production and global biogeochemical cycles. Their interactions with NNPs and ENPs is therefore paramount for the understanding of their environmental fate. An important factor is the colloidal stability of the particles that determines their retention time and a release of its components which can change the mechanism of interaction with the environment. Early studies made by Mahendra et al. (2008), showed that bacteria (Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) degrade CdSe/ZnS SQDs under acidic or alkaline (pH 2 and 12) conditions. Degradation processes removed the coatings of the QDs and had bactericidic effects that were attributed to the release of Cd, Se, and Zn ions. Since no uptake was observed, it was assumed that the toxicity originated from oxidative stress. The authors also observed that organic ligands, such as naturally abundant humic acids, lowered the bactericidic effects. In previous studies, the metal release from SQDs by photooxidation (Derfus et al., 2004) or degradation was also reported and the toxicity of QDs was associated to their uptake into the cells (Chang et al., 2006).

The diameter of nanoparticles (core: 1–100 nm) relative to the pore diameters of the plant cell wall (plasmodesmata), which exhibits diameters of 3.5–5 nm (Peters et al., 2021), indicate a geometric restriction for penetrating the plant cell via active or passive transport. However, mechanisms resembling endocytosis or nonendocytic penetration allow the absorption of larger nanoparticles (Navarro et al., 2012). To date, internalization of nanoparticles does not indicate immediate phytotoxicity because plants have physical defence structures (i.e., exclusion through cell wall, sequestration in vacuole, etc.), as well as chemical defense structures (i.e., regulation of different plant proteins – glutathione, phytochelatins, etc.) that allow them to adapt to different types of environmental stressors (Navarro et al., 2012).

However, glycine-, mercaptosuccinic acid-, cysteine-, and cysteamine-conjugated CdSe/ZnS SQDs were reported to be taken up from aqueous solutions and were tracked in the vasculature of ryegrass (Lolium perenne), onion (Allium cepa) and chrysanthemum (Chrysanthemum sp.) (Al-Salim et al., 2011). The study showed that the root epidermis was the limiting zone for nearly all investigated QD types. However, other authors found a transport though the roots of annual bluegrass (Poa annua) of these types of QDs, when the roots were colonized by an arbuscular mycorrhizal fungus (Whiteside et al., 2009). Therefore, Al-Salim et al. (2011) concluded that the fungus could have modified the coating of the SQDs and made it more mobile. Leafroller larvae from the family Tortricidae were fed with these QDs for two to four days and showed fluorescence along the entire gut, in the larval feces, and in their haemolymph. The fluorescent SQDs were also detected after metamorphosis in the adults (Al-Salim et al., 2011).

Uptake of SQDs with cationic compared to anionic coatings were investigated by CLSM and ICP-MS for the poplar tree Populus deltoides × nigra, which was hydroponically exposed to 50 nM CdSe/CdZnS SQDs coated with cationic polyethylenimine (PEI) (35.3 ± 6.6 nm) or poly(ethylene glycol) of anionic poly(acrylic acid) (PAA-EG) (19.5 ± 7.2 nm) (Wang et al., 2014). Uptake of cationic PEI-QDs was faster than of anionic PAA-EG-QDs, which was attributed to the electrostatic repulsion by the negatively charged cell wall. However, no significant translocation was reported. In turn, a transformation was observed that suggested that part of the organic coating on the SQDs’ surface was lost inside the plant, which led to particle aggregation and inhibited the mobility. The authors proposed that QD coatings could be susceptible to transformation, conjugation, and sequestration processes used by plants to process xenobiotic contaminants.

The fate of the mixtures of CdS/ZnS SQDs with different surface coatings on mangrove (Kandelia obovata) and wheat root (Triticum aestivum) epidermis were investigated using NTFS (Li et al., 2019). The technique allowed to reveal that transportation and biodegradation were the main factors for the dissipation of CdS/ZnS SQDs at the root epidermis. The Cd quantity in the roots was determined by ICP-MS and was used to determine the transportation rate of the CdS/ZnS SQDs. However, after exposure for 8 h, CdS/ZnS SQDs formed large cluster in both the intra- and intercellular space of root epidermis cells that led to accumulation. Additionally, the authors claimed that selective transportation processes could be involved in the depuration process after 7 days. In addition, it was shown that the uptake by the root epidermis of mangrove species (Kandelia obovata, Avicennia marina, and Avicennia corniculata) was much higher for hydrophobic oleylamine-CdS/ZnS SQDs, than for cationic PEG–COOH–CdS/ZnS SQDs, and the least for anionic PEG–NH2–CdS/ZnS SQDs. The authors showed that the retention of CdS/ZnS SQDs on the epidermal tissues was determined by multiple factors, which included the composition of mangrove root, endocytosis, and the charge of the CdS/ZnS SQDs' coating. However, it was observed in an earlier work that the degradation of the SQDs to metal ions was caused by rhizophytic bacteria at the surface of the roots, which lowered the concentrations to a larger extent than the transportation process into the plant (Li et al., 2019). The retention of PEG–NH2–CdS/ZnS SQDs was slightly higher than that of PEG-COOH–CdS/ZnS which was attributed to the electrostatic attraction of positively charged coatings to the negatively charged root cell wall, while oleylamine-CdS/ZnS SQDs were suggested to be transported via the xylem/phloem and were located on the epidermal tissues of mangrove roots. PEG–COOH–CdS/ZnS and MPA–COOH–CdS/ZnS SQDs retained longer at epidermis and were also found to evoke negative physiological response (Li et al., 2019).

Direct comparisons of the metal salt (CdCl2) with the Cd-based SQDs capped with the two ligands glutathione (GSH) or 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were tested for toxicity and bioaccumulation for freshwater model plant common duckweed (Lemna minor), which is a bioindicator species for the detection and monitoring of metal pollution and bioaccumulation (Modlitbová et al., 2018b). The authors showed that GSH- and MPA-capped SQDs had similar toxicity but were both less toxic than CdCl2. However, both sources of Cd led to similar patterns of Cd bioaccumulation and distribution. LIBS and ICP-OES analyses suggested that the coatings became unstable and Cd ions, that leached from the QD core, were causing toxic effects. The study concluded that the main mediators of Cd toxicity and bioaccumulation in aquatic plants are Cd ions dissolved from Cd-based SQD core materials.

Navarro et al. (2012) showed that intact polymer-coated (carboxylate-terminated) core/shell CdSe/ZnS SQDs in the model plant thale cress (Arabidopsis thaliana) were not transported or uptaken by plant root surfaces as shown by fluorescence microscopy within 7 days of exposure. The potential root uptake and transport of water-dispersible CdSe/ZnS SQDs was investigated by determining the Cd and Se concentrations in roots and leaves by ICP-MS. Control experiments with the soluble forms (Cd2+ and SeO3 2−) showed that these ions were translocated to the leaves indicating the transport from the roots. Ions can be released from SQDs due to organic acids present in soil and water that are derived from natural organic matter. Despite no evidence of nanoparticle internalization, gluththione levels (GSH and GSSG) suggest that SQDs caused oxidative stress when plants were exposed to SQD dispersions containing humic acids (Navarro et al., 2012). Other researchers showed also that carboxylate-terminated CdSe/ZnS SQDs were not taken up by annual bluegrass (Poa annua) (Whiteside et al., 2009).

On the contrary, Koo et al. (2015) reported the intact QD uptake into roots and translocation to leaves of Arabidopsis thaliana and even further the subsequent ingestion by insects (Trichoplusia ni), which was revealed by fluorescent microscopy. The roots of A. thaliana were exposed to CdSe/CdZnS SQDs with poly(acrylic acid-ethylene glycol (PAA-EG) and polyethylenimine (PEI) coatings and showed strong evidence that intact SQDs were taken up and transported to the leaves.Detectable levels of Cd and Se were also found in insects and frass. However, the detected Cd not be distinctly attributed to intact or dissolved QDs.

Toxicity and bioaccumulation of core, core/shell, and core/shell/shell SQDs (CdTe, CdTe/ZnS, and CdTe/CdS/ZnS SQDs capped by 2-mercaptopropionic acid) was also tested on onions (Allium cepa). CdTe/ZnS SQDs showed no toxicity and very low bioaccumulation of Cd in A. cepa, while CdTe/CdS/ZnS and CdTe SQDs showed higher toxicity and bioaccumulation, which indicated unstable CdS/ZnS shells in the latter two cases (Modlitbová et al., 2018c). The ZnS shell encapsulation effectively reduced the release of Cd ions from core in aqueous media and also the generation of free radicals by the SQDs due to oxidation (Derfus et al., 2004; Modlitbová et al., 2018b). The highest concentration of Cd was found on the roots of A. cepa where QDs were adsorbed.

In another study, the toxicity, bioaccumulation, and translocation of SQDs and Cd salt in white mustard (Sinapis alba) revealed that CdTe/SiO2 SQDs was five times less prone to be taken up by plants than the control samples (CdTe QDs or CdCl2) from the aqueous solution (Modlitbová et al., 2020). However, Cd ions from the SiO2 shell-coated core were translocated from the roots to the entire plant and released toxic ions during their transport, which suggests that CdTe/SiO2 SQDs are toxic for plants (Modlitbová et al., 2020). Commonly, the silica coating of QDs were shown to successfully inhibit the leakage of metal ions into the environment, but can increase toxicity in plants as more Cd is translocated. In addition, such an increased stability could lead to accumulatory effects that cause larger toxicity after throphic transfer, when it is digested and the silica shell is lost due to changing chemical conditions (e.g. acidic conditions in the stomach).

The fate of CdS SQDs in A. thaliana (wildtype) and two mutant lines, was investigated by EXAFS to assess the potential of bio- and phytoremediation of nanoparticles (Marmiroli et al., 2020). The mutant lines, that were known to be tolerant to cadmium-based CdS SQDs but not to CdSO4, were exposed to CdS SQDs or CdSO4 for 20 days. Once taken up systemically in the plant, the SQDs underwent a biotransformation process, where SQDs remained largely bound to different organic molecules and did not release free ions. In contrast, CdSO4 was taken up directly, which was more toxic. The biotransformation process was associated with stress response of the plants that traps QDs rather than dissolving them and reduces therefore the toxicity towards the plants, which is an important finding for the use of plants for phytoremediation (Ebrahimbabaie et al., 2020). However, such accumulations can lead to more toxic effects when the material is ingested by plant eating animals.

CQDs have emerged as a less toxic alternative to inorganic heavy metal-based SQDs, but there is a lack of information and understanding regarding the environmental effects of these materials, such as its transport, fate, and long-term impact (Peralta-Videa et al., 2020). A study by Li et al. (2018) demonstrated the individual impact of five oxygen-containing graphene-derived CQDs on rice plants using microscopy imaging. The CQDs were found to penetrate all parts of the plant, including the cell nuclei, and enhance the rice-plants disease resistance ability by increased gene expression.

With regard to the application of SQDs for fluorescence labelling, an interesting approach is the use of SQDs as pollen labels to track the fate of individual pollen grains (Minnaar and Anderson, 2019). The authors successfully tested the applicability of CuInSexS2 − x/ZnS (core/shell) SQDs with oleic-acid ligands, which they regarded as non-toxic labels and suggested to use this novel approach to study pollen movement and distribution in the environment.

The behavior and empirical determination of the predicted environmental concentrations of ENPs remain a still challenging field in risk assessment, and knowledge on their fate is still undertaken mainly in laboratory experiments. Although QDs cannot mimic all types of ENPs these materials remain important for many experiments as they can be tracked easily by a large set of analytical techniques. More recently, isotope labelling was introduced for SQDs, which increased the limit of detection to the ppt level and allows to study their behavior at more relevant environmental concentrations. The QD's unique spectroscopic properties are still important to study the influence of the protective shells or the coating. However, many QD-based nanotracers were not fully characterized, as the photoluminescence quantum yield is in some cases not declared. However, this information is essential to determine the limit of detection when using, for example, fluorescence methods for QD detection and quantification, which may be a source of contradictory results. Therefore, profound information obtained from physical and chemical characterization should be generally declared in a standardized procedure, as e.g. implemented for the safety dossiers according to the amendment of Registration, Evaluation, Authorization and Restriction of Chemicals (REACH) (European Parliament and of the Council, 2006; European Commission, 2018, 2020). Such sdossiers must be set for chemicals of more than one ton per year (production or import volume), with exceptions for chemicals below that threshold or such that are used for research. Although, these dossiers are not mandatory for most QDs due to these exceptions, it would be beneficial to provide the physical-chemical properties in a standardized way in scientific publications. Better characterized materials will allow to obtain more precise data which in turn results in a better understanding of the environmental fate of ENPs.

Given the low toxicity from the elemental composition of CQDs and GQDs, these types of nanotracers may be used in field experiments in the future. However, these materials are limited, as they are currently not applicable with high sensitivity methods that rely on the quantitation of elements or isotopes, as CQDs and GQDs consist mainly of omnipresent carbon. In addition, further studies on their long-term toxicity are required.

The application of QD-based nanotracers in water bodies, soil and plants showed that the coatings of ENPs plays a major role, as they determine the uptake and accumulation of ENPs or of leached dissolved ions in the matrix, which has consequences on further transfers. Case studies using QD-based nanotracers showed that ENPs interact with plant roots in many ways, such as adsorption onto the root surface, uptake via the root, transport within the plants, and incorporation into the cell wall. Moreover, it was shown that presence and type of microorganisms in soil can also determine or accelerate the dissolution of QDs before they enter plants. Systematic research, into the junctions (e.g., soil, microorganisms and plants using nanotracers, such as surface modifiable QDs, is necessary to close the knowledge gaps and result in stability parameters and fate descriptors that help to understand the behavior of ENPs in the soil-plant system. This may help to anticipate future scenarios of exposure and support risk assessment of nano-enabled products.

Anna Pavlicek: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing Simon Neubauer: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Investigation, Christian Zafiu: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Investigation, Visualization, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Marion Huber-Humer: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Eva-Kathrin Ehmoser: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Florian Part: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Investigation, Visualization, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.
